claimed_contribution: Provides a brief editor-in-chief update on the operational and scholarly state of Journal of Marketing Research.
Reports concrete journal performance indicators (submission volume and geography, acceptance rate, review turnaround, impact factor, and subscription mix).
Announces funded page expansions to reduce backlog, two upcoming special issues, and editorial office personnel changes.
context: The article is an editorial update about the Journal of Marketing Research during 2010, with some forward-looking details for 2011-2012. It concerns journal management, submissions, review outcomes, readership, and planned special issues.
design_and_data: This is a descriptive editorial report, not a hypothesis-testing study. The author summarizes internal journal statistics, including submissions from July 1, 2009, to June 30, 2010 (459 new submissions from 39 countries) and year-to-date 2010 submissions through October 19, 2010 (452, with an expected approximately 550 for the full year). It reports process and outcome metrics such as an approximately 13% annual acceptance rate, 61 days average submission-to-decision time for new manuscripts (excluding desk rejects and acceptances), a 2009 impact factor without self-cites of 2.9, and subscription composition percentages.
main_findings: All 2010 JMR issues were published with 50% more pages, which the editor states significantly alleviated backlog, and AMA funding will support similarly expanded February and April 2011 issues.

JMR received 459 new submissions from 39 countries between July 1, 2009, and June 30, 2010, and had 452 new submissions by October 19, 2010, implying an expected approximately 550 submissions for calendar year 2010.

Reported performance indicators include an approximately 13% annual acceptance rate, a 61-day average decision time for new manuscripts (excluding desk rejects and acceptances), a 2009 impact factor without self-cites of 2.9, and a subscription base with substantial practitioner representation.
one_line_summary: This editorial provides a metrics-based status update on JMR's publication capacity, submission and review performance, readership profile, and upcoming special issues.
research_question: What is the current state of Journal of Marketing Research under the present editorship, as reflected in backlog management, submission trends, review performance, journal impact, readership composition, and planned editorial initiatives?
